text: The surgeon makes a small incision, approximately four to five centimeters, located above the umbilicus and below the xiphisternum, to access the distal stomach where the growth is situated in the antrum and pylorus region.

The surgeon makes a small incision to assess the operability of the disease, visually inspecting the peritoneal surfaces and omentum for deposits. The liver surface and mesenteries of the small and large bowel are examined for any visible signs of disease.
The surgeon extends the incision up to the xiphisternum. The transverse colon is held in the surgeon's left hand, while the assistant holds the omentum and stomach.

The surgeon carefully separates the transverse colon and its mesentery from the stomach and omentum, ensuring no heat transmission to the large bowel. This dissection is performed along the transverse colon and near the splenic flexure.
The surgeon separates the stomach from its attachment to the colon near the hepatic flexure, ensuring complete detachment from the transverse colon and surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs caucarization to separate the stomach from the colon and duodenum, with the gallbladder visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates visible structures near the stomach, which is being separated from surrounding tissues, potentially affected by H. pylori infection.
The surgeon observes a growth in close contact with the capsule, proceeding to dissect and remove part of the pancreas and subpyloric nodes as part of the surgical field.

The surgeon has separated the duodenum from the stomach, ensuring an adequate margin of more than one centimeter. A DST stapler is used to cut and separate the growth from the stomach and duodenum. The surgeon palpates the area to confirm the adequacy of the margins.
The surgeon staples the duodenal stump and proceeds to place a protective layer over it, carefully taking a layer from the pancreatic capsule.

The surgeon carefully dissects part of the pancreas, focusing on the serous and muscularis layers. The falciform ligament is freed and placed over the duodenal stump, providing an additional protective layer.
The surgeon places a protective flap of ligament over the duodenal stump to localize and cover the area, ensuring it remains secure and promotes healing.
The surgeon continues the dissection at the proximal end, ensuring a minimum margin of four centimeters from the tumor. The visual field shows a clear area with no visible tumor growth nearby.
The surgeon highlights the junction violet marking on the gastric tissue, indicating the precise location for incision during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes marking the stomach after palpating to ensure adequate margins are maintained around the tumor site.
The surgeon positions the NG tube away from the staple line and proceeds along the lesser curvature of the stomach, preparing to ligate the left gastric artery and coronary vein.

The surgeon performs a D2 lymph node dissection, removing lymph nodes along the hepatic artery, splenic artery, left gastric artery, and celiac area. Concurrently, the left gastric artery and coronary vein are ligated.

The surgeon completes the detooling from dissection, ligating the vessels after removing the lymph nodes. The area where the stomach will be cut is prepared, and a green cartridge is used for stapling.
The surgeon delineates the area along the greater curvature of the stomach near the gastro-splenic ligament, preparing to cut the stomach while retracting the omentum.
The surgeon preserves the short gastric vessels while working near the gastro-splenic ligament.
The surgeon uses a linear cutter with a green DST cartridge to transect the stomach along the delineated greater curvature, ensuring precise cutting and stapling.
The surgeon approaches the lesser curvature of the stomach, using two intestinal clamps instead of a stapler to economize. A knife or scissors is used to cut the tissue, leaving an opening.
The surgeon displays the excised specimen, including the distal part of the stomach, omentum, and DII lymph nodes, preparing to anastomose the stomach to the jejunum along the greater curvature side.
The surgeon uses suction to prevent spillage and reinforces the stapled end of the stomach with trio silk sutures, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon reinforces the staple line by suturing the seromuscular layers of the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach, ensuring additional support to the stapled area.
The surgeon continues reinforcing the staple line on the stomach, ensuring additional security against potential leaks.
Following the completion of the gastric resection, the surgeon holds the jejunal end in the left hand and staples it approximately fifteen centimeters from the duodenojejunal flexure.
The surgeon uses an NTLC stapler to join the proximal and distal ends of the gastrointestinal tract, with the proximal end positioned to the right and the distal end to the left.
The surgeon reinforces the proximal and distal ends with threosilk sutures and anastomoses the distal jejunal loop to the stomach.
The surgeon creates a gastrojejunostomy by connecting the remnant stomach to the jejunum, ensuring the continuity of the gastrointestinal tract for food passage.
The surgeon holds the colon and creates an opening in the mesentery to the right of the middle colic vessels. Through this opening, the distal loop is prepared for gastrointestinal anastomosis.

The surgeon marks the area on the stomach with blue dye, indicating where the jejunum will be anastomosed. The gastro-jejunal anastomosis is initiated, with the marked site approximately three to four centimeters in size.

The surgeon takes the fourth layer by suturing the seromuscular layer of the posterior wall of the stomach to the seromuscular layer of the anterior wall of the jejunum using a continuous 3-0 silk suture.
The surgeon positions the surgical field approximately one to two centimeters from the cut end of the posterior wall of the stomach, aligning similarly on the jejunal side for the anastomosis.
The surgeon opens the lumen of the jejunum to match the size of the stomach lumen, preparing to start the third layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses Vicryl 3-0 sutures to perform a continuous suturing technique, starting from the jejunal end. The needle is inserted from the serosa into the mucosa and then back from the mucosa to the serosa, ensuring secure attachment of the jejunum to the stomach.
The surgeon inserts the needle into the stomach wall, passing from the serosa to the mucosa and then back from the mucosa to the serosa, completing the suturing technique.
The surgeon ties the first anchoring knot at the junction between the stomach and jejunum, securing the initial connection in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the anastomosis between the stomach and the jejunum, aligning the two lumens for connection.
The surgeon passes the suture from the serosa to the mucosa of the jejunum, continuing the anastomosis process.

The surgeon takes a full-thickness bite of both the stomach and jejunum, passing the suture from the serosa to the mucosa of each structure. The needle is visible entering the serosa of the stomach and exiting through the mucosa, then entering the serosa of the jejunum and exiting through the mucosa.

The surgeon inserts the suture needle into the mucosa of the stomach, then through the serosa, followed by the serosa of the jejunum and into its mucosa, creating continuous sutures along the third layer of the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the third layer of sutures in the anastomosis between the stomach and jejunum, ensuring the continuous stitching reaches the end of the layer.
The surgeon begins suturing the second layer of the anastomosis between the stomach and jejunum, following the completion of the third layer.
The surgeon knots the suture and begins placing conal sutures for the second layer, focusing on the junction between the stomach and jejunum.

The surgeon demonstrates suturing technique by entering from the serosa to the mucosa and then returning from the mucosa to the serosa on the same side. A Ryles tube is positioned to guide the placement of the second layer of sutures over it.
The surgeon demonstrates the technique of placing conal sutures, ensuring the mucosa and serosa remain intact without protrusion during the anastomosis.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of conal sutures by passing the needle from the serosa to the mucosa and then back from the mucosa to the serosa on the same side, illustrating the stitching pattern along the tissue line.

The surgeon reaches the end of the suture line, leaving two to three kernels loose to ensure visibility and avoid incorporating the posterior wall in the bites, aiming for adequate thickness of the serosa and mucosa layers.
The surgeon approaches the completion of the second layer of sutures, ensuring proper alignment and tension in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon focuses on the fourth layer, highlighting the seromuscular layer of the jejunum and the seromuscular part of the stomach.
The surgeon reinforces the second layer of the surgical site, focusing on securing the sutures in the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon completes the gastrojejunal anastomosis using a 3-0 silk suture, continuing with the same needle and suture from the fourth layer. The sutures are anchored to avoid tension at the edges of the anastomosis.
The surgeon secures the jejunal loop by placing two to three sutures at the rent through which the loop was passed, ensuring stability after completing the gastrojejunal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a jejuno-jejunal anastomosis, located fifty centimeters from the gastrojejunal anastomosis, connecting the proximal jejunal loop to the distal segment. The fourth layer of sutures is being applied to reinforce the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses 3-0 silk sutures to perform an anastomosis, ensuring it is appropriately sized for the passage of bile from the liver and pancreatic enzymes, maintaining continuity without requiring a large opening.
The surgeon creates a hole in the tissue, ensuring it is adequately sized for the limbs of the surgical stapling device to enter and perform the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a cutting cautery device to make precise incisions in the tissue.
The surgeon uses an instrument to create a hole in the artery, preparing for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon enters the limb through the previously created hole, preparing for the next step in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a jejunal-jejunal anastomosis, connecting two segments of the jejunum using surgical techniques appropriate for this procedure.
The surgeon closes a small rent in the tissue, likely using sutures or a stapling device, ensuring the integrity of the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to close the tissue layers.

The surgeon performs conal sutures by inserting the needle from the serosa to the mucosa on one side and then returning from the mucosa to the serosa on the same side before moving to the opposite side.
The surgeon demonstrates the conal suture technique, passing the needle from the serosa to the mucosa and then back from the mucosa to the serosa, completing the stitch on the opposite side.
The surgeon completes the duodenojejunal anastomosis, visually confirming the connection between the duodenum and jejunum.
The surgeon performs a pharyngeal genostomy, manipulating the tissue in the pharyngeal region to create an opening.

The surgeon places a drain over the duodenal stump and the gastrojejunostomy closure, positioned approximately fifteen centimeters distal to the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis to bypass the anastomosis and allow healing.
The surgeon uses a loop rolling technique to handle nylon sutures, preparing for closure or securing structures within the surgical field.
